original: I lowered Will’s chair, got him inside, and made him a warm drink. I changed his shoes and trousers, put the beer-stained ones in the washing machine, and got the fire going. I put the television on, and drew the curtains so that the room grew cozy around us—perhaps cozier for the time spent out in the cold air. But it was only when I sat in the living room with him, sipping my tea, that I realized he wasn’t talking—not out of exhaustion, or because he wanted to watch the television. He just wasn’t talking to me.
paraphrase: I moved Will's chair into a reclined position, brought him indoors, and prepared a hot beverage for him. I swapped his shoes and pants, placed the beer-soiled garments into the washing machine, and started a fire. I switched on the television and closed the curtains, making the room feel snug around us—possibly even more inviting after our time spent in the chilly outdoor air. However, it was not until I was settled in the living room beside him, enjoying my tea, that I became aware of his silence—not due to fatigue, or because his attention was focused on the television. He simply wasn't speaking to me.

original: Okonkwo ate the food absent-mindedly. 'She should have been a boy,' he thought as he looked at his ten-year-old daughter. He passed her a piece of fish. "Go and bring me some cold water," he said. Ezinma rushed out of the hut, chewing the fish, and soon returned with a bowl of cool water from the earthen pot in her mother's hut. Okonkwo took the bowl from her and gulped the water down. He ate a few more pieces of plantain and pushed the dish aside. "Bring me my bag," he asked, and Ezinma brought his goatskin bag from the far end of the hut.
paraphrase: Okonkwo consumed his meal with a distracted mind. He mused, 'If only she had been born a son,' while observing his daughter, who was ten years old. He offered her a portion of fish. "Fetch me some chilled water," he instructed. Ezinma quickly exited the dwelling, masticating the fish, and promptly reappeared carrying a container of refreshing water, sourced from the clay vessel in her mother's living space. Okonkwo accepted the container from her and rapidly drank the water. He ingested several additional slices of plantain before shoving the plate away. "Retrieve my satchel for me," he requested, and Ezinma retrieved his goatskin satchel from the opposite side of the dwelling.

original: Langdon accepted the pencil from Bellamy and tapped it on his chin as he studied the cipher. The code was so simple that he barely needed pencil and paper. Even so, he wanted to ensure he made no mistakes, and so he dutifully put pencil to paper and wrote down the most common decryption key for a Masonic cipher. The key consisted of four grids—two plain and two dotted—with the alphabet running through them in order. Each letter of the alphabet was now positioned inside a uniquely shaped “enclosure” or “pen.” The shape of each letter’s  enclosure  became the  symbol  for that letter.

The simplicity of the code meant he hardly required writing implements. Nevertheless, he was determined to avoid any errors, so he conscientiously began writing with the pencil on the paper, transcribing the most frequently used decryption key for a Masonic cipher. This key comprised four distinct grids—two that were plain and two that featured dots—with the entire alphabet sequentially arranged within them. Consequently, every letter of the alphabet was now situated within a distinctively shaped "enclosure" or "pen." The specific shape of each letter's enclosure then served as the unique "symbol" representing that particular letter.

original: A decade younger than I was now. Had Stahl offered her a ride? Had he dragged her into the woods, hidden her away where no one could find her? I shuddered. I understood what Cass meant, now, when she said she wanted to tell Persephone that shed had a daughter. Hes dead, I wanted to whisper to those bones. Id found Persephone, just as Liv had, and this must be how Liv had felt, too, like she had searched the underworld for her ghost and sighted her at last. Jessi wasnt Persephone but Eurydice, and Liv was Orpheus, guiding her back toward the surface only tofoolishly, inevitablylook back as she had been forbidden to do, and now both of them were lost below.
paraphrase: I was ten years younger at that moment. Could Stahl have offered her a ride? Did he pull her into the forest, concealing her where she couldn't be discovered by anyone? A shiver ran through me. I now grasped the meaning behind Cass's statement, when she expressed her desire to inform Persephone that she had given birth to a daughter. "He's deceased," I wished I could murmur to those skeletal remains. I had located Persephone, exactly as Liv had, and this sensation must have mirrored Liv's experience, too, as if she had scoured the underworld for Persephone's spectral presence and finally caught sight of her. Jessi wasn't Persephone, but rather Eurydice, and Liv embodied Orpheus, leading her back towards the world above, only to—with foolish inevitability—glance behind her, an act she had been prohibited from doing, and consequently, both of them were now lost in the depths below.

original: I heard of him first in rather a romantic manner, from a lady who owes to him the happiness of her life. This, briefly, is his story. Some years ago he loved a young Russian lady of moderate fortune, and having amassed a considerable sum in prize-money, the father of the girl consented to the match. He saw his mistress once before the destined ceremony; but she was bathed in tears, and throwing herself at his feet, entreated him to spare her, confessing at the same time that she loved another, but that he was poor, and that her father would never consent to the union.

original: Grenouille sat at his ease on his bench in the cathedral of Saint-Pierre and smiled. His mood was not euphoric as he formed his plans to rule humankind. There were no mad flashings of the eye, no lunatic grimace passed over his face. He was not out of his mind, which was so clear and buoyant that he asked himself why he wanted to do it at all. And he said to himself that he wanted to do it because he was evil, thoroughly evil. And he smiled as he said it and was content. He looked quite innocent, like any happy person.

original: “Refreshments! Refreshments,” roared the old man. Everyone began eating, and whatever hothouses your people may have, you have never tasted such grapes. Really good grapes, firm and tight on the outside, but bursting into cool sweetness when you put them into your mouth, were one of the things the girls had never had quite enough of before. Here, there were more than anyone could possibly want, and no table-manners at all. One saw sticky and stained fingers everywhere, and, though mouths were full, the laughter never ceased nor the yodeling cries of  Euan, euan,  eu-oi-oi-oi-oi , till all of a sudden everyone felt at the same moment that the game (whatever it was), and the feast, ought to be over, and everyone flopped down breathless on the ground and turned their faces to Aslan to hear what he would say next.
paraphrase: "Food and drink! Food and drink!" bellowed the elderly gentleman. All present started to partake, and regardless of how advanced your greenhouses might be, you have never sampled grapes like these. Truly exceptional grapes, externally firm and compact, yet exploding with a cool sweetness upon consumption, were something the young women had previously found themselves lacking. Here, the abundance exceeded anyone's desires, and all notions of etiquette were cast aside. One observed sticky and discolored digits throughout the gathering, and despite mouths being full, the merriment never subsided, nor did the joyful yelps of "Euan, euan, eu-oi-oi-oi-oi," until unexpectedly, everyone simultaneously sensed that the activity (whatever its nature) and the meal should conclude. Consequently, everyone collapsed, panting, onto the earth, directing their gazes towards Aslan, eager to learn his subsequent pronouncement.

original: Later this sunroom would become something different. The forest was vast and wild. He doubted theyd ever be able to find him there. On sunny days, a herd of deer would graze on the grass below him, and the birds would sing above him. He hummed along with their song. Giovanni was at peace. At peace until the day his chest began to hurt. Oh my, he said. What an interesting sensation. It burns. In his lab he ran calculations. He typed on his keyboard, the clack, clack, clack echoing flatly around him. I see, he said on the fifty-second day after hed first felt the ache in his chest.

On days blessed with sunshine, a group of deer would leisurely feed on the verdant grass beneath his vantage point, while avian melodies resonated above him. He joined in with their vocalizations, humming contentedly. Giovanni experienced a profound sense of tranquility. This state of peace persisted until the moment his chest started to ache. "Goodness gracious," he uttered, "what a peculiar feeling this is." He noted, "It has a burning quality." Within the confines of his laboratory, he diligently performed computations. His fingers moved across his keyboard, producing a rhythmic "clack, clack, clack" that reverberated dully throughout the space. "Understood," he declared on the fifty-second day subsequent to his initial experience of the discomfort in his chest.

Desperate and empty and confused people might choose such an existence. Such a choice will lead not to Shangri-la, but to a vortex of madness. Avatars do not make a community; they are shadows of people, not people. Mile by mile, Michael more fully understands the truth of the future that could overtake him, and he is chilled to his core.

To inhabit such an artificial construct is akin to being interred while still alive, yet without the peace that death offers. Individuals who are desperate, feeling hollow, or disoriented might opt for such a way of life. However, this particular decision will not culminate in an idyllic paradise, but rather in a spiraling descent into insanity. Digital representations or "avatars" do not constitute a true community; instead, they are mere semblances of individuals, not actual human beings. Incrementally, Michael grasps with greater clarity the genuine nature of the potential future that could engulf him, and a profound coldness permeates his very being.

original: They had plodded on for about half an hour (three of them very stiff from yesterday’s rowing) when Trumpkin suddenly whispered, “Stop.” They all stopped. “There’s something following us,” he said in a low voice. “Or rather, something keeping up with us: over there on the left.” They all stood still, listening and staring till their ears and eyes ached. “You and I’d better each have an arrow on the string,” said Susan to Trumpkin. The Dwarf nodded, and when both bows were ready for action the party went on again.
paraphrase: After trudging onward for approximately thirty minutes, with three members of the group feeling quite sore from the previous day's rowing, Trumpkin abruptly uttered a hushed "Stop." Everyone immediately halted. "Something is pursuing us," he declared in a subdued tone. "More precisely, something is paralleling our movement: it's over there to the left." They all remained motionless, straining their ears to listen and their eyes to observe until the effort caused discomfort. "It would be wise for you and me to each nock an arrow," Susan suggested to Trumpkin. The Dwarf gave a nod of agreement, and once both bows were prepared for use, the group resumed their journey.

'You would serve me best, lady,' replied the girl, wringing her hands, 'if you could take my life at once; for I have felt more grief to think of what I am, to-night, than I ever did before, and it would be something not to die in the hell in which I have lived.

The girl, with a dismissive wave of her hand, responded, "Not a single cent." Rose, taking a soft step forward, implored, "Please do not be unreceptive to my attempts to assist you. I genuinely desire to help you." The girl, twisting her hands together, countered, "You could aid me most effectively, madam, if you were to end my life immediately; because the sorrow I feel tonight, contemplating my current state, surpasses any grief I have previously experienced, and it would be a blessing not to perish in the torment I have endured throughout my existence."

Least you don't know all about that thing. 'Tain't robbery altogether -- it's revenge!" and a wicked light flamed in his eyes. "I'll need your help in it. When it's finished -- then Texas. Go home to your Nance and your kids, and stand by till you hear from me.

original: He was getting, he judged, three meals in the twenty-four hours; sometimes he wondered dimly whether he was getting them by night or by day. The food was surprisingly good, with meat at every third meal. Once there was even a packet of cigarettes. He had no matches, but the never-speaking guard who brought his food would give him a light. The first time he tried to smoke it made him sick, but he persevered, and spun the packet out for a long time, smoking half a cigarette after each meal. They had given him a white slate with a stump of pencil tied to the corner.

original: Everyone stopped talking to watch. Malfoy smiled nastily. "I think I'll leave it somewhere for Longbottom to find -- how about -- up a tree?" "Give it here!" Harry yelled, but Malfoy had leapt onto his broomstick and taken off. He hadn't been lying, he could fly well. Hovering level with the topmost branches of an oak he called, "Come and get it, Potter!" Harry grabbed his broom. "No!" shouted Hermione Granger. "Madam Hooch told us not to move -- you'll get us all into trouble." Harry ignored her. Blood was pounding in his ears. He mounted the broom and kicked hard against the ground and up, up he soared; air rushed through his hair, and his robes whipped out behind him -- and in a rush of fierce joy he realized he'd found something he could do without being taught -- this was easy, this was wonderful.
paraphrase: All conversation ceased as everyone directed their attention to the scene. Malfoy's grin was malicious. "My plan is to hide it somewhere for Longbottom to discover -- perhaps -- atop a tree?" "Hand it over!" Harry demanded, but Malfoy had already launched himself onto his broomstick and ascended. His prior boast wasn't an exaggeration; he possessed considerable flying skill. As he hovered at the same height as the highest branches of an oak, he taunted, "Come and retrieve it, Potter!" Harry seized his broom. "Don't!" Hermione Granger exclaimed. "Madam Hooch instructed us not to move -- you'll cause problems for everyone." Harry disregarded her warning. The sound of his own pulse throbbed in his ears. He straddled the broom, pushed off forcefully from the ground, and ascended higher and higher; the wind whipped through his hair, and his school robes billowed behind him -- and with a surge of intense delight, he recognized he had discovered an ability he could perform naturally -- this was effortless, this was magnificent.

original: The image I kept returning to was of a tangled necklace chain. In one of the more normal moments of my later childhood, my mother taught me to rub a chain with olive oil, then take a long, straight pin and start working on the tiniest of gaps, the place with the most give. Once one thing loosened, another could loosen, another. I always felt claustrophobic at the start. But over time Id learned patience, learned the reward of breathing through my discomfort. What I knew was that wed found a gap in the knot. I didnt know what else it would loosen up, and I didnt want to pull too hard, but I knew if we finessed it, wiggled it gently, other things would follow.

original: They altered course and made for this land, mostly by oar, for the wind would not serve them to sail northeast. When evening fell they were still a long way from it and rowed all night. The next morning the weather was fair but a flat calm prevailed. The dark mass lay ahead, much nearer and larger, but still very dim, so that some thought it was still a long way off and others thought they were running into a mist.
paraphrase: Changing their direction, they headed towards this land, primarily propelled by oars, as the wind was unhelpful for a northeast sailing journey. On the following morning, the atmospheric conditions were pleasant, yet a complete absence of wind persisted. The obscured landmass appeared in front of them, noticeably closer and more expansive, though still quite indistinct. This led some to believe it was still far away, while others conjectured they were approaching a fog bank.

original: "Eh!" he said, "that sounds as if tha'd got wits enow. Tha'rt a Yorkshire lad for sure. An' tha'rt diggin', too. How'd tha' like to plant a bit o' somethin'? I can get thee a rose in a pot." "Go and get it!" said Colin, digging excitedly. "Quick! Quick!" It was done quickly enough indeed. Ben Weatherstaff went his way forgetting rheumatics. Dickon took his spade and dug the hole deeper and wider than a new digger with thin white hands could make it. Mary slipped out to run and bring back a watering-can. When Dickon had deepened the hole Colin went on turning the soft earth over and over.
paraphrase: "Well, well!" he exclaimed, "that suggests you possess ample intelligence. You are certainly a Yorkshire boy, no doubt about it. And you're engaged in digging as well. How would you feel about planting a little something? I could procure a potted rose for you." "Go and retrieve it immediately!" Colin declared, digging with great enthusiasm. "Hurry! Hurry!" The task was indeed accomplished with remarkable speed. Ben Weatherstaff departed, his joint pains forgotten. Dickon seized his spade and enlarged the excavation, making it both deeper and broader than a novice digger with delicate, pale hands could possibly manage. Mary stealthily exited to retrieve a watering-can and return with it. After Dickon had further deepened the cavity, Colin continued to meticulously turn over the loosened soil repeatedly.

original: I pressed too hard on the ice dispenser and the cup overflowed, the shock of the ice on my skin a reminder to reel my imagination back in. “Back then, we had a connection. A . . . flirtation,” I said, busying myself filling two glasses with ice. “And thinking back on it is destined to be a little weird since I was working for you at the time, but now I respect your position on the board.” I finally met his eyes again as I walked back into the room and handed him the cold glass; our fingers touched for a moment, a beat too long, before he pulled away.
paraphrase: Applying excessive pressure to the ice dispenser caused my cup to overflow, and the sudden chill of the ice against my skin served as a jolt, pulling my thoughts back to reality. "In those days, there was a bond between us. A certain... chemistry," I explained, deliberately focusing on filling two tumblers with ice. "And revisiting those memories is bound to feel a bit awkward given that I was your employee at that time, but currently, I hold great respect for your role on the board." My gaze finally reconnected with his as I re-entered the room and extended the chilled glass to him; our fingertips brushed for an instant, a fraction of a second more than necessary, before he withdrew his hand.

original: “I wish you to pay attention to what I am going to say to you. The colts who live here are very good colts, but they are cart-horse colts, and, of course, they have not learned manners. You have been well bred and well born; your father has a great name in these parts, and your grandfather won the cup two years at the Newmarket races; your grandmother had the sweetest temper of any horse I ever knew, and I think you have never seen me kick or bite. I hope you will grow up gentle and good, and never learn bad ways; do your work with a good will, lift your feet up well when you trot, and never bite or kick even in play.”
paraphrase: "I want you to listen carefully to the words I am about to speak. The young horses residing here are exceptionally fine, but they are specifically cart-horse colts, and, as such, they have naturally not acquired proper etiquette. You, however, have been raised with excellent breeding and have a distinguished lineage; your father is highly renowned in this region, and your grandfather achieved victory in the cup at the Newmarket races for two consecutive years. Your grandmother possessed the most amiable disposition of any horse I have ever encountered, and I believe you have never witnessed me engaging in kicking or biting. My hope is that you will mature into a graceful and virtuous animal, and never adopt undesirable habits; perform your tasks diligently, lift your hooves properly when you are trotting, and refrain from biting or kicking under any circumstances, even during playful interactions."

Selleck's voice carried an air of indifference, as if this were merely a routine occurrence. Viv remained silent, but offered a subtle, wordless acknowledgment. We've been struck." Viv swallowed hard. "Hit?" "Yes," Selleck confirmed, "but it's nothing more than a minor collision."

original: Do you remember the night? Cranly lost his temper and began to talk about Wicklow bacon. --I remember, said Lynch. He told us about them flaming fat devils of pigs. --Art, said Stephen, is the human disposition of sensible or intelligible matter for an esthetic end. You remember the pigs and forget that. You are a distressing pair, you and Cranly. Lynch made a grimace at the raw grey sky and said: --If I am to listen to your esthetic philosophy give me at least another cigarette. I don't care about it. I don't even care about women. Damn you and damn everything.
paraphrase: That evening, do you recall it? Cranly became enraged and started discussing Wicklow bacon. "I do remember," Lynch responded. "He went on about those fiery, fat pig devils." "Art," Stephen declared, "is the arrangement of perceptible or understandable material by humans for an aesthetic purpose. You recollect the pigs, yet you've forgotten that. You two, you and Cranly, are truly exasperating." Lynch scowled at the bleak, overcast sky and stated, "If I am to endure your aesthetic theories, at the very least provide me with another cigarette. I have no interest in it. I have no interest in women either. To hell with you and to hell with everything else."

original: It was the old New York way of taking life "without effusion of blood": the way of people who dreaded scandal more than disease, who placed decency above courage, and who considered that nothing was more ill-bred than "scenes," except the behaviour of those who gave rise to them. As these thoughts succeeded each other in his mind Archer felt like a prisoner in the centre of an armed camp. He looked about the table, and guessed at the inexorableness of his captors from the tone in which, over the asparagus from Florida, they were dealing with Beaufort and his wife.
paraphrase: This represented the traditional New York approach to handling life's difficulties, characterized by avoiding any public display of emotion or conflict, or "without effusion of blood." It was the method favored by individuals who feared public disgrace more intensely than illness, who prioritized proper conduct over bravery, and who believed that nothing was more uncouth than creating public "scenes," with the sole exception being the conduct of those who caused such disturbances. As these reflections cycled through his thoughts, Archer experienced a sensation akin to being a captive situated within the heart of a fortified encampment. He surveyed the assembled company around the table, and from the firm manner in which they were discussing Beaufort and his spouse while consuming asparagus sourced from Florida, he deduced the unyielding resolve of those who held him metaphorically captive.

original: And he made a dive for his desk, grabbing paper, ink, and a fresh handkerchief, laid it all out properly, and began his analysis. The procedure was this: to dip the handkerchief in perfume, pass it rapidly under his nose, and extract from the fleeting cloud of scent one or another of its ingredients without  being significantly distracted by the complex blending of its other parts; then, holding the handkerchief at the end of his outstretched arm, to jot down the name of the ingredient he had discovered, and repeat the process at once, letting the handkerchief flit by his nose, snatching at the next fragment of scent, and so on.…
paraphrase: He launched himself towards his desk, securing paper, ink, and a new handkerchief, meticulously arranging them, and then commenced his examination. His method involved several steps: first, immersing the handkerchief in perfume, swiftly moving it beneath his nose, and identifying a specific component from the transient scent cloud without being unduly influenced by the intricate combination of its other elements. Next, he would hold the handkerchief at arm's length, record the name of the ingredient he had identified, and immediately recommence the procedure, allowing the handkerchief to quickly pass his nose, capturing the subsequent olfactory fragment, and continuing in this manner.

original: We dance all night, stopping only for me to down my liquor-free mojito in three desperate gulps. They really are delicious; I love the mint. I am drenched in sweat, and I know that my curls have fallen and my stringy hair is clinging to my forehead. He picks me up bridal style and whirls me around to the beat when my feet  finally give out on me. He is dauntless. The music makes him come alive; nothing can stop him. On this island, with the music running through my veins, I feel the weight of all that has happened slide away, if just for now. I am so light that I swear I could take flight like one of the gulls that ride the wind, hovering between water and sky.
paraphrase: Throughout the entire night, we continue dancing, pausing only when I rapidly consume my non-alcoholic mojito in three urgent swallows. These drinks are genuinely delightful; I particularly enjoy the mint flavor. My body is soaked with perspiration, and I am aware that my once-curled hair has straightened and my lank strands are stuck to my forehead. When my legs eventually can no longer support me, he lifts me in a bridal hold and twirls me in rhythm with the music. He possesses an indomitable spirit. The music invigorates him completely; there is nothing that can impede him. On this island, with the music flowing through my entire being, the burden of all past events seems to dissipate, even if only temporarily. I feel so incredibly buoyant that I genuinely believe I could ascend into the air, much like the gulls that glide on the breeze, suspended between the ocean and the vast expanse of the sky.

original: He had gone to see him, with the doctor, after their conversation. The old man had greeted Tarrou with grimaces and by rubbing his hands together. He was in bed, propped up on his pillow, leaning over his two pots of chickpeas. “Ah! Another one!” he said when he saw Tarrou. “The world is upside-down, more doctors than patients. It’s because it moves fast, eh? The priest was right, we deserve it.” The next day, Tarrou went back unannounced.
paraphrase: Following their discussion, he, accompanied by the doctor, had visited the old man. The old man had welcomed Tarrou with a series of facial contortions and by vigorously rubbing his hands together. He lay in his bed, supported by his pillow, and was hunched over his two containers of chickpeas. Upon seeing Tarrou, he exclaimed, “Oh! Not another one!” He continued, “The natural order is inverted; there are more medical professionals than sick people. This is due to its rapid progression, isn’t it? The clergyman spoke truly, we are deserving of this fate.” Tarrou returned the subsequent day without prior notification.

Harry put on his pajamas, took off his glasses, and climbed into his chilly bed while Ron threw Owl Treats up on top of the wardrobe to pacify Hedwig and Pigwidgeon, who were clattering around and rustling their wings restlessly.

original: It was an argument she had with herself daily, sometimes more than once, always with the same outcome. There were no jobs to be found in Dinan, or Rennes for that matter, certainly nothing that paid as well as her position at the Hôtel Ritz. It was settled, then. There would be—could be—no escape for Camille. Only duty. Obligation. And perhaps, in time, restitution, for the past, for herself, and for the lives she’d ruined with a single thoughtless act. And if a part of her was relieved she wouldn’t have to face what she’d done, well, that was between herself and her own conscience.
paraphrase: She engaged in an internal debate on a daily basis, occasionally multiple times within a single day, and each time the conclusion was identical. Employment opportunities were nonexistent in Dinan, and the situation in Rennes was no different; there was definitely nothing available that offered a comparable salary to her role at the Hôtel Ritz. Consequently, the decision was final. There was no possibility—and indeed, could be no possibility—of Camille escaping her current circumstances. Her path was solely one of duty and obligation. Furthermore, perhaps eventually there would be atonement for her past actions, for her own sake, and for the lives she had irrevocably damaged through one careless deed. And if a part of her felt a sense of relief at not having to confront the consequences of her actions, then that was a matter strictly confined to her personal thoughts and moral compass.

original: The beef is delicious and served with roasted sweet potatoes, carrots, parsnips, and green beans.  It is even more palatable since Christian manages to retain his good-humor for the rest of the meal.  I suspect that it’s because I’m eating so heartily.  The conversation flows freely among the Greys, warm and caring, gently teasing each other.  Over our dessert of lemon syllabub, Mia regales us with her exploits in Paris, lapsing at one point into fluent French.  We all stare at her, and she stares back puzzled, until Christian tells her in equally fluent French what she’s done, whereupon she bursts into a fit of giggles.  She has a very infectious laugh and soon we’re all in stitches.

original: The Countess rounded on him. “I can’t do this? The custodium is your responsibility, Jorais. Its failings are yours. I may have approved Tazah’s promotion, but you made the suggestion. And while I knew you proposed him in order to buy his silence, you never knew why  I  agreed. So many little accidents while you were dragging the family name through the Gutterfields’ mire. So many secrets to be kept and bodies to be weighted down. Tazah knew them all. His reports were very thorough. And eminently verifiable.” The anger faded from her expression until she simply looked tired. “You should have supported me just now, Jorais.”
paraphrase: The Countess turned abruptly towards him. “Are you suggesting I am incapable of this? The responsibility for the custodium falls squarely on your shoulders, Jorais. Any shortcomings it exhibits are your fault. It is true that I gave my approval for Tazah's promotion, but the initial recommendation came from you. And even though I was aware that your motivation for proposing him was to secure his silence, you were never privy to the reasons behind my concurrence. There were so many minor incidents that occurred while you were sullying the family's reputation in the Gutterfields’ squalor. So many confidences that needed to be guarded and remains that had to be submerged. Tazah possessed knowledge of every single one of them. His comprehensive reports were incredibly detailed. And undeniably confirmable.” The fury gradually receded from her countenance, leaving her appearing utterly exhausted. “You ought to have stood by me at this very moment, Jorais.”

original: “It took everything I had not to jump up in the middle of that class full of children and—” He stopped abruptly, looking away. “When you walked past me, I could have ruined everything Carlisle has built for us, right then and there. If I hadn’t been denying my thirst for the last, well, too many years, I wouldn’t have been able to stop myself.” He paused, scowling at the trees.

Is it that you lift your left leg number 28 and then your right leg number 39? Or do you begin by lifting your right leg number 17 before you lift your left leg number 44? I await your answer in breathless anticipation. Yours truly, Tortoise.”

original: Bond heard slow steps approaching behind his chair. 'Dhrop it,' said the voice. Bond saw Le Chiffre's hand open obediently and the knife fall with a clatter to the floor. He tried desperately to read into Le Chiffre's face what was happening behind him, but all he saw was blind incomprehension and terror. Le Chiffre's mouth worked, but only a high-pitched 'eek' came from it. His heavy cheeks trembled as he tried to collect enough saliva in his mouth to say something, ask something. His hands fluttered vaguely in his lap. One of them made a slight movement towards his pocket, but instantly fell back.
paraphrase: Slow footsteps drew nearer from the direction behind his seat, and Bond heard them. The voice commanded, "Dhrop it." Bond observed Le Chiffre's hand release its grip as instructed, and the knife descended, striking the floor with a sharp noise. He made a frantic effort to decipher from Le Chiffre's facial expression what events were unfolding out of his sight, but he perceived only a vacant bewilderment and dread. Le Chiffre's lips parted and moved, yet only a shrill "eek" emitted from them. His substantial jowls quivered as he attempted to gather sufficient moisture in his oral cavity to articulate a statement or pose a question. His hands hovered indistinctly in his lap. One hand made a subtle gesture towards his pocket, but immediately withdrew.

original: Not on the water. Under it. A light. What kind of light? Not our kind. Tetu took off his backpack and pulled out a small antique mahogany box, an incongruous item set among emergency rations and survival equipment. As though presenting a gift, he opened the lid of the box and Echo leaned forward, peering at fragments of alien technology, a collection veined with capillaries of mauve and jade, which continued to pulse despite being broken and separate from any obvious power source. She picked up the largest of these shards, bringing the light close to her eyes. The law states that all alien discoveries must be turned over to McMurdo.
paraphrase: Not floating on the water, but submerged beneath its surface, there was a light. What type of illumination was it? It wasn't anything we recognized as ours. Tetu unshouldered his backpack and retrieved a small, aged mahogany container, an item that seemed out of place amidst the emergency food supplies and survival gear. He opened the lid of the box as if offering a present, and Echo inclined her body forward, intently examining pieces of extraterrestrial technology. These fragments were crisscrossed with delicate, thread-like patterns of purple and green, and they continued to emit light even though they were broken and clearly disconnected from any visible power supply. She selected the largest of these fractured pieces, holding the radiant object near her eyes. According to regulations, all discoveries of alien origin are mandated to be delivered to McMurdo.

original: During the time I lived in Heidelberg I commuted to Frankfurt four times a week to see my analyst. The ride took an hour each way and trains became an important part of my fantasy life. I kept meeting beautiful men on the train, men who scarcely spoke English, men whose clichés and banalities were hidden by my ignorance of French, or Italian, or even German. Much as I hate to admit it, there are  some  beautiful men in Germany.

I continually encountered attractive individuals on the train, specifically men who communicated very little in English. original: In this he was not disappointed. A few days  later his Tutor said, “Tonight I am going to give you a lesson in Astronomy. At dead of night two noble planets, Tarva and Alambil, will pass within one degree of each other. Such a conjunction has not occurred for two hundred years, and your Highness will not live to see it again. It will be best if you go to bed a little earlier than usual. When the time of the conjunction draws near, I will come and wake you.”
paraphrase: His expectations were met. Several days afterward, his instructor stated, "Tonight, I will provide you with a lesson concerning Astronomy. Precisely at midnight, two magnificent planets, Tarva and Alambil, are scheduled to approach within one degree of one another. An alignment of this nature has not taken place for two centuries, and Your Highness will not experience it again in your lifetime. It would be most advisable for you to retire to bed somewhat sooner than your customary time. As the moment of the conjunction approaches, I shall arrive to rouse you from sleep."

original: I told myself that I should leave it alone, that once the Fairy got hold of Drosselmeyer, whatever he had intended would not matter. We had a made a bargain, the Fairy and I, and though the time had not yet come for fulfillment, I thought I would know when it did. Yet when I saw Drosselmeyer beneath my window, hat tipped down to shadow his one eye, it suddenly seemed to me that the past was no longer shut in its high cupboard, that the strange Christmas Eve in my memory had been a piece of a story, a much greater story of which Clara and I were only a small part, and that if Drosselmeyer was still watching me, still waiting, then I was not out of the story yet.
paraphrase: I initially resolved to disregard the situation, believing that once the Fairy gained control of Drosselmeyer, his original plans would become irrelevant. A pact had been established between the Fairy and me, and despite the fact that its execution was not yet due, I assumed I would recognize the moment of its arrival. However, upon observing Drosselmeyer below my window, his hat angled to obscure his sole eye, a sudden realization struck me: the past was no longer confined to its distant storage. The peculiar Christmas Eve I recalled now appeared to be a mere segment of a more extensive narrative, a grander tale in which Clara and I played only minor roles. And if Drosselmeyer continued to observe me, patiently anticipating, then my involvement in that narrative had clearly not concluded.

original: "You should know," said Estella. "I am what you have made me. Take all the praise, take all the blame; take all the success, take all the failure; in short, take me." "O, look at her, look at her!" cried Miss Havisham, bitterly; "Look at her, so hard and thankless, on the hearth where she was reared! Where I took her into this wretched breast when it was first bleeding from its stabs, and where I have lavished years of tenderness upon her!" "At least I was no party to the compact," said Estella, "for if I could walk and speak, when it was made, it was as much as I could do.

"Oh, observe her, observe her!" exclaimed Miss Havisham, with profound bitterness; "Behold her, so unyielding and unappreciative, standing by the very fireplace where she was brought up! The very place where I embraced her into this sorrowful heart when it was initially wounded by its betrayals, and where I have generously bestowed countless years of affection upon her!" "At the very least, I was not involved in the agreement," Estella responded, "because if I possessed the ability to walk and speak when that arrangement was formed, that was the absolute extent of my capabilities at that time."

original: Yes, you. Not me. You. Because youre a private person and I know youve walked red carpets a few times, but I dont think youre used to being ambushed in normal life like I am. In front of us, the ocean appeared again, turquoise beneath a cornflower sky, and the beauty of the view was an unpleasant contrast to the disagreeable energy inside the car. At least sixty seconds had passed when, taking pains to keep my tone matter-of-fact rather than resentful, I said, Theres nothing you promised me. Im complicit in how undefined whatever were doing is. I admit that.
paraphrase: Indeed, it is you, not I. This is because you value your privacy, and while I recognize your experience with red carpet events, I don't believe you are accustomed to the spontaneous and intrusive encounters that are a regular part of my life. Before our eyes, the ocean re-emerged, its vibrant turquoise hues set against a sky the color of cornflowers. The sheer splendor of this vista starkly contrasted with the uncomfortable atmosphere pervading the vehicle. After at least a minute had elapsed, I spoke, making a deliberate effort to sound objective rather than bitter, stating, "You made no commitments to me. I acknowledge my involvement in the ambiguous nature of our current situation. I concede this point."

original: Hunter told me that all he could see on the pages of my manuscript was a jumble of letters and numbers floating on the pages, and trying to make out the words stressed him out to the point of actual nosebleeds. Books gave him anxiety and that was why he kept putting off reading mine. I quickly understood what he was telling me—he was dyslexic—but I didn’t get how he’d made it into a thirty-grand-a-year prep school without help, until he reminded me of the arsenal of sports trophies in his father’s den. Hunter finally wiped his cheeks with his sleeve as he told me the truth about where he got the slit in his left brow. It was the first time I understood why he had become so protective of me after I told him I was being slammed into lockers and dumped into trash cans.
paraphrase: Hunter informed me that his perception of my manuscript's content was solely a chaotic mix of letters and digits suspended on the pages. He explained that his attempts to decipher the words caused him such immense stress that he would experience genuine nosebleeds. Literature, he clarified, induced anxiety in him, which was the reason he continuously postponed reading my work. I swiftly grasped his meaning – he had dyslexia – but I couldn't comprehend how he had managed to attend a prep school costing thirty thousand dollars annually without assistance. Then, he brought to my attention the extensive collection of sports trophies displayed in his father's study. Hunter, having wiped his face with his sleeve, eventually revealed the true story behind the laceration above his left eyebrow. It was at this juncture that I first comprehended the reason for his heightened protectiveness towards me, which began after I disclosed that I was being forcibly pushed into lockers and thrown into trash receptacles.
